name: dumbbell incline shoulder raise
instructions: Set an incline bench to a 45-degree angle and sit on it with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing down.
Rest the dumbbells on your thighs and lean back onto the bench.
Use your thighs to help raise the dumbbells to shoulder height, then rotate your wrists so that your palms are facing forward.
Exhale and slowly raise the dumbbells above your head, keeping a slight bend in your elbows.
Pause for a moment at the top, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Set an incline bench to a 45-degree angle and sit on it with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing down. Rest the dumbbells on your thighs and lean back onto the bench. Use your thighs to help raise the dumbbells to shoulder height, then rotate your wrists so that your palms are facing forward. Exhale and slowly raise the dumbbells above your head, keeping a slight bend in your elbows. Pause for a moment at the top, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
